column1: "What does an income elasticity of > 1 (e.g., 1.6) indicate about medical care?","It indicates that medical care is a superior or luxury good at the aggregate level, meaning as people get richer, they spend a disproportionately higher percentage of their wealth on health [1]."

"What is the overall price elasticity of medical care, and what does it mean?","The overall price elasticity is roughly -0.2 (inelastic), meaning a 10% increase in out-of-pocket costs only reduces demand by 2% [1]."

"What were the main questions the RAND Health Insurance Experiment sought to answer?","The experiment asked whether higher cost-sharing reduces medical spending, and more importantly, whether it ruins health [1]."

"According to the RAND Experiment, which type of medical service is the most inelastic (urgently needed regardless of price)?","Inpatient hospital services are highly inelastic, with a price elasticity of -0.1 [1]."

"According to the RAND Experiment, which type of medical service is highly elastic?","Dental services are highly elastic (-0.4), meaning patients are highly likely to defer dental care if out-of-pocket costs rise [1]."

"Why is cost-sharing considered a 'blunt instrument' according to the RAND Experiment?","Because patients lack the medical expertise to separate necessary from unnecessary care, higher cost-sharing reduced both appropriate and inappropriate care in roughly equal proportions [1]."

"How did high cost-sharing affect the health outcomes of the average, relatively healthy person in the RAND Experiment?","There was no significant difference in health outcomes; for the average person, cost-sharing was purely a financial issue, not a health issue [1]."

"How did cost-sharing affect vulnerable (poorest and sickest) populations in the RAND Experiment?","It acted as a dangerous barrier to essential care, leading to worse health outcomes, whereas free care led to significant improvements like better-controlled hypertension and better dental health [1]."
